title: Assessment: Complexity Analysis — Big-O, Master Theorem, Amortised

questions.question: f(n) = 3n² + 100n + 500. Which of the following is the correct Big-O classification?

options.A: O(3n²) because the leading coefficient matters
options.B: O(n² + n) because lower-order terms affect growth for small n
options.C: O(n²) — dominant term only, constant factors and lower-order terms dropped
options.D: O(n³) — always round up to a higher order for safety

questions.explanation: Big-O drops constant factors and lower-order terms. For large n, 3n² dominates 100n and 500. Constants 3, 100, 500 are absorbed by the c in the formal definition. O(n²) is the correct tightest Big-O upper bound.

questions.question: A coding problem states n ≤ 10⁶. Your current solution is O(n log n). Will it pass within 1 second (assuming ~10⁸ simple operations/second)?

options.A: No — n log n with n=10⁶ ≈ 2×10⁷ operations, which exceeds 10⁸
options.B: Yes — n log n with n=10⁶ ≈ 2×10⁷ operations, well within 10⁸
options.C: Impossible to determine without knowing the constant factor
options.D: No — O(n log n) is too slow for n > 10⁴

questions.explanation: n=10⁶, log₂(10⁶) ≈ 20. n log n ≈ 2×10⁷ operations — well within the ~10⁸ budget. O(n²) = 10¹² would TLE. O(n log n) is the standard sorting-based upper limit that passes for n up to ~10⁷.

questions.question: Apply the Master Theorem to T(n) = 4T(n/2) + O(n). What is the tight bound?
questions.explanation: a=4, b=2, log₂(4)=2. Compare f(n)=O(n)=O(n¹) to n^log_b(a) = n². Since n¹ = O(n^(2-ε)) for ε=1, Case 1 applies: T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)) = Θ(n²). The recursive subproblems dominate.

questions.question: A binary search tree's height is O(log n) for a balanced tree but O(n) for a skewed tree. What does this imply about BST lookup complexity?

options.A: BST lookup is always O(log n) — the binary search property ensures this
options.B: BST lookup is O(h) where h is the height — O(log n) for balanced, O(n) worst-case for skewed
options.C: BST lookup is Θ(log n) average across all possible insertions
options.D: BST lookup is O(1) amortised for red-black trees regardless of height

questions.explanation: BST lookup traverses one root-to-node path of length h. Balanced BST (AVL, red-black): O(log n). Skewed insertion order (1,2,3,4,5 into a BST): degenerates to a linked list, h=n, O(n) lookup.

questions.question: Union-Find with both path compression and union by rank has O(α(n)) amortised complexity. In practice for all feasible n, α(n) ≤ 5. What does this mean for interview complexity discussions?

options.A: State that DSU is O(1) amortised since α(n) is so small it is negligible
options.B: State O(α(n)) precisely — interviewers know this notation and it signals rigour
options.C: Always state O(log n) as a conservative safe estimate
options.D: Avoid DSU in interviews since its complexity is too complex to explain

questions.explanation: Saying O(α(n)) is precise and expected for DSU. Saying O(1) is technically wrong (it's a soft approximation). O(log n) overstates the cost. Using the correct notation signals understanding of amortised analysis and the inverse Ackermann function.

questions.question: Which of the following best distinguishes Θ (Theta) from O (Big-O)?

options.A: O gives the exact running time; Θ gives the worst-case upper bound
options.B: O is an upper bound (at most); Θ is both upper and lower (exactly this growth rate, up to constant)
options.C: Θ adds constant factors that O drops; they differ only for small n
options.D: O and Θ are interchangeable; industry uses O colloquially to mean Θ

questions.explanation: O(g): f ≤ c·g (upper bound). Ω(g): f ≥ c·g (lower bound). Θ(g): f is both O(g) and Ω(g) — tight bound. In practice, interviewers say 'O(n log n)' but often mean Θ. Knowing the distinction — and being precise when asked — demonstrates theoretical rigour.

questions.question: The amortised cost of Python list.append() is O(1). A colleague argues: 'But occasionally it is O(n), so the amortised claim is misleading.' How do you respond?

options.A: You agree — O(1) amortised is just a mathematical shortcut that hides the real cost
options.B: You explain: amortised analysis accounts for the infrequent O(n) resizes. The per-operation average over n appends is O(1) — a worst-case guarantee on the average, not an approximation
options.C: You agree that append is O(n) worst-case and recommend pre-allocating lists
options.D: Amortised analysis is approximate; the colleague is correct to be skeptical

questions.explanation: Amortised O(1) is a rigorous guarantee: the total cost of n appends is O(n) (table doubling ensures each element is copied O(1) times on average). It's not approximate — infrequent expensive operations are paid for by the 'credit' saved during cheap operations.

questions.question: State the three cases of the Master Theorem for T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) and give a concrete example algorithm for each case.

required_points: Case 1: f(n) = O(n^(log_b(a) - ε)) → T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a)). Example: naive Strassen 4T(n/2)+O(n) = Θ(n²)
Case 2: f(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) · log^k(n)) → T(n) = Θ(n^log_b(a) · log^(k+1)(n)). Example: merge sort 2T(n/2)+O(n) = Θ(n log n)
Case 3: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b(a) + ε)) and regularity condition → T(n) = Θ(f(n)). Example: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(n) = Θ(n)
answer_key.acceptable_variations: Exact wording of conditions may vary; any correct algorithm example for each case accepted
answer_key.full_credit: All three cases correctly stated with an example for each
answer_key.partial_credit: Two cases correctly stated with examples

questions.question: A function's inner loop runs O(log n) times for each of the n outer iterations: `for i in range(n): j=n; while j>1: j//=2`. State the total complexity and justify your answer with an explicit counting argument.

required_points: Inner while loop halves j each time → runs log₂(n) iterations
Outer for loop runs n times
Total: n × log₂(n) = O(n log n)
Justification: operations at each outer step = log₂(n); summing over all n steps = n·log n
answer_key.acceptable_variations: May write as sum ∑ₙ log n = n log n
answer_key.full_credit: Correct O(n log n) with explicit multiplication argument

questions.prompt: Why is amortised analysis more useful than worst-case analysis for data structures like dynamic arrays and Union-Find? Describe a scenario where relying on worst-case analysis alone would lead you to choose a worse data structure.
questions.guidance: Strong answer: worst-case for a single append is O(n) → suggests always using linked lists; amortised shows O(1) per append making arrays competitive. For DSU: log n per operation (no optimisations) vs O(α(n)) amortised with both optimisations — wrong data structure choice if comparing log n DSU to better alternatives.

rubric.A: Defines amortised vs worst-case clearly, gives concrete data structure example where worst-case misleads, identifies the correct data structure choice using amortised reasoning
rubric.B: Explains the concept correctly but gives only one example or the example is vague
rubric.C: Describes the difference between amortised and worst-case without applying it to a concrete data structure decision
